content.text: Raw data is rarely ready to train on. Before a model can learn anything useful, it needs data that is complete, consistent, correctly typed, and shaped to surface the signal the model actually needs. In production ML pipelines this preprocessing work is repetitive, error-prone, and — when done manually — a leading source of train/serve skew. A data preprocessing agent automates the full pipeline: it profiles the incoming dataset, decides which transformations to apply, applies them in a leakage-free order, engineers additional features, selects the most informative ones, and serializes the resulting sklearn-compatible pipeline so the exact same transformations run at inference time.

This is not a simple script. The agent must reason about data characteristics — missingness mechanisms, distribution shapes, cardinality of categoricals, temporal structure — and choose transformations that are appropriate for each column rather than applying blanket rules. It must also enforce strict ordering: fit all transformers on the training split only, then apply them to validation and test. Getting that order wrong silently inflates evaluation metrics, a class of bug that only surfaces after a bad production deployment.
What It Is

A data preprocessing agent is an autonomous component in an ML pipeline that accepts a raw dataset and a task specification (target column, problem type, compute budget) and produces a clean, feature-engineered, split dataset together with a serialized, versioned sklearn Pipeline. Rather than relying on a human to manually inspect data and hard-code transformations, the agent reasons about the dataset's statistical profile and selects the appropriate treatment for each column type.
Profiles each column: dtype, cardinality, null rate, skewness, kurtosis, min/max
Classifies missing data mechanism (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and selects imputation strategy accordingly
Detects and treats outliers using IQR fencing, Z-score, or Isolation Forest depending on distribution shape
Chooses scaler (Standard, MinMax, Robust) based on whether the model is distance-based and whether outliers are severe
Encodes categoricals using OHE for low-cardinality, target encoding for high-cardinality, ordinal for ordered
Engineers new features: polynomial interactions, date decomposition, ratio features
Selects features using mutual information and SHAP importance from a fast proxy model
Splits data with leakage-free ordering: fit transformers only on the training fold
Why It Matters

Data quality determines a ceiling on model performance that no architecture or hyperparameter can breach. Studies consistently find that data scientists spend 60–80% of their project time on data preparation. Automating it frees that capacity for higher-value work like problem framing and post-deployment analysis. More importantly, a pipeline agent enforces correctness rules — particularly the train/test split discipline — that humans routinely violate under time pressure, often without noticing until evaluation metrics mismatch production metrics.

A serialized pipeline artifact also solves the training-serving skew problem. When the same Pipeline object that transformed training data is deployed alongside the model, every inference request passes through identical transformations. Without this, teams must maintain parallel preprocessing code — one for training notebooks and one for the serving layer — which diverges silently over time.
Benefits
Eliminates training-serving skew: same Pipeline object runs at both training and inference
Prevents data leakage by construction: the agent enforces fit-on-train-only discipline
Reproducibility: versioned artifacts mean every model run can be exactly reproduced
Accelerates experimentation: the agent handles boilerplate so engineers iterate on modeling, not plumbing
Generates a data quality report surfacing issues the downstream model cannot fix: impossible values, unexpected cardinality shifts, label imbalance
Adaptive to column types: handles numeric, categorical, datetime, text columns without manual dispatch
Trade-offs
Automated decisions can be wrong: the agent may choose median imputation where domain knowledge calls for a constant sentinel value
Feature engineering search space is combinatorial: the agent must bound the search or it creates thousands of low-value features
Target encoding introduces its own leakage risk if not implemented with proper cross-fold encoding
Large datasets make profiling expensive: sampling heuristics are needed to keep runtime reasonable
Versioned pipelines add storage overhead: each run produces a new artifact that must be managed in a registry
How It Works

The agent begins by profiling the raw DataFrame: for each column it computes null rate, unique count, inferred dtype, distribution shape statistics (skewness, kurtosis), and — for numeric columns — checks for near-constant variance. This profile drives all downstream decisions. A column with 80% nulls is dropped rather than imputed. A numeric column with skewness above 2 is log-transformed before scaling. A categorical column with over 50 unique values gets target encoding rather than one-hot encoding.

After profiling, the agent performs the train/val/test split — this happens before any fitting so that no statistics computed on the training set can contaminate validation or test. The split is stratified for classification tasks. It then fits all transformers (imputers, scalers, encoders) exclusively on the training fold and applies transform to validation and test. The fitted transformers are assembled into a single sklearn ColumnTransformer wrapped in a Pipeline and serialized with joblib. The Pipeline is logged to MLflow as a named artifact tied to the training run, making it retrievable by run ID at serving time.
Architecture

The agent is structured as a multi-step planner. The Profiler reads the raw DataFrame and emits a column manifest: a structured record of each column's type, null rate, cardinality, and distribution statistics. The TransformPlanner reads the manifest and emits a list of Transform directives — one per column — specifying the exact sklearn transformer to apply. The PipelineBuilder assembles the directive list into a ColumnTransformer, wraps it in a Pipeline, and fits it on the training split. The FeatureEngineer runs as a separate pass before fitting, adding polynomial and interaction columns to the DataFrame. The FeatureSelector runs after fitting the pipeline, using SHAP values from a lightweight LightGBM proxy model to prune features whose importance is below a threshold. Finally, the ArtifactManager serializes the final Pipeline, writes a data quality report, and registers both in MLflow.
When to Use
Tabular datasets with mixed column types, missing values, or outliers that need systematic treatment
Pipelines where training-serving skew has previously caused production regressions
Teams running frequent retraining (daily or more) where manual preprocessing review is impractical
AutoML setups where feature engineering is part of the search space
Regulated environments where reproducibility of every model version must be auditable
How to Implement

The following implementation shows the core preprocessing agent. It profiles each column, decides on transformers, builds a ColumnTransformer, fits only on the training split, and serializes the pipeline to MLflow.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import joblib
import mlflow
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Tuple
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer, KNNImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, RobustScaler, OneHotEncoder, TargetEncoder
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split


@dataclass
class ColumnProfile:
    name: str
    dtype: str
    null_rate: float
    n_unique: int
    skewness: float = 0.0
    is_target: bool = False


@dataclass
class TransformDirective:
    column: str
    steps: List  # list of (name, transformer) tuples


def profile_dataframe(df: pd.DataFrame, target: str) -> List[ColumnProfile]:
    profiles = []
    for col in df.columns:
        null_rate = df[col].isna().mean()
        n_unique = df[col].nunique(dropna=True)
        dtype = "numeric" if pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df[col]) else "categorical"
        skewness = float(df[col].skew()) if dtype == "numeric" else 0.0
        profiles.append(ColumnProfile(
            name=col,
            dtype=dtype,
            null_rate=null_rate,
            n_unique=n_unique,
            skewness=skewness,
            is_target=(col == target),
        ))
    return profiles


def plan_transforms(profiles: List[ColumnProfile]) -> List[TransformDirective]:
    directives = []
    for p in profiles:
        if p.is_target or p.null_rate > 0.8:
            continue  # drop near-empty columns; target handled separately
        steps = []
        if p.dtype == "numeric":
            imputer = KNNImputer(n_neighbors=5) if 0.05 < p.null_rate <= 0.3 else SimpleImputer(strategy="median")
            steps.append(("impute", imputer))
            scaler = RobustScaler() if abs(p.skewness) > 2 else StandardScaler()
            steps.append(("scale", scaler))
        else:
            steps.append(("impute", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")))
            if p.n_unique > 50:
                steps.append(("encode", TargetEncoder()))
            else:
                steps.append(("encode", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False)))
        directives.append(TransformDirective(column=p.name, steps=steps))
    return directives


def build_column_transformer(directives: List[TransformDirective]) -> ColumnTransformer:
    transformers = []
    for d in directives:
        pipe = Pipeline(steps=d.steps)
        transformers.append((d.column, pipe, [d.column]))
    return ColumnTransformer(transformers=transformers, remainder="drop")


def run_preprocessing_agent(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    target: str,
    test_size: float = 0.2,
    val_size: float = 0.1,
    random_state: int = 42,
) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, Pipeline]:
    # 1. split FIRST — before any profiling that could leak statistics
    X = df.drop(columns=[target])
    y = df[target].values
    X_train_val, X_test, y_train_val, y_test = train_test_split(
        X, y, test_size=test_size, random_state=random_state, stratify=y if y.dtype == object else None
    )
    X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
        X_train_val, y_train_val,
        test_size=val_size / (1 - test_size),
        random_state=random_state,
    )

    # 2. profile training split only
    profiles = profile_dataframe(X_train.assign(**{target: y_train}), target=target)

    # 3. plan and build transformer
    directives = plan_transforms(profiles)
    col_transformer = build_column_transformer(directives)
    pipeline = Pipeline(steps=[("preprocessing", col_transformer)])

    # 4. fit on train only, transform all splits
    X_train_proc = pipeline.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)
    X_val_proc = pipeline.transform(X_val)
    X_test_proc = pipeline.transform(X_test)

    # 5. serialize pipeline and log to MLflow
    with mlflow.start_run(run_name="preprocessing", nested=True):
        artifact_path = "/tmp/preprocessing_pipeline.joblib"
        joblib.dump(pipeline, artifact_path)
        mlflow.log_artifact(artifact_path, artifact_path="pipeline")
        mlflow.log_params({
            "n_features_in": X_train.shape[1],
            "n_features_out": X_train_proc.shape[1],
            "n_directives": len(directives),
        })

    return X_train_proc, X_val_proc, X_test_proc, pipeline
Examples
The following example shows how to add a feature engineering pass before the main transformer, creating polynomial and date-decomposed features from a raw DataFrame.

from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin


class DateDecomposer(BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin):
    """Expand datetime columns into year, month, day, dayofweek, hour."""
    def __init__(self, cols):
        self.cols = cols

    def fit(self, X, y=None):
        return self

    def transform(self, X):
        X = X.copy()
        for col in self.cols:
            dt = pd.to_datetime(X[col], errors="coerce")
            X[f"{col}_year"] = dt.dt.year
            X[f"{col}_month"] = dt.dt.month
            X[f"{col}_dayofweek"] = dt.dt.dayofweek
            X[f"{col}_hour"] = dt.dt.hour
            X = X.drop(columns=[col])
        return X


def engineer_features(df: pd.DataFrame, numeric_cols: list, date_cols: list) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add polynomial interactions for numerics and decompose date columns."""
    df = DateDecomposer(cols=date_cols).fit_transform(df)
    if numeric_cols:
        poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=2, interaction_only=True, include_bias=False)
        poly_matrix = poly.fit_transform(df[numeric_cols])
        poly_names = poly.get_feature_names_out(numeric_cols)
        poly_df = pd.DataFrame(poly_matrix, columns=poly_names, index=df.index)
        # keep only interaction terms (containing " ") to avoid redundant columns
        interaction_cols = [c for c in poly_names if " " in c]
        df = pd.concat([df, poly_df[interaction_cols]], axis=1)
    return df
The feature selector below uses a fast LightGBM proxy model to compute SHAP importances and prunes features below the 5th percentile, then removes highly correlated feature pairs.

import shap
import lightgbm as lgb


def select_features(
    X_train: np.ndarray,
    y_train: np.ndarray,
    feature_names: list,
    importance_threshold_pct: float = 5.0,
    corr_threshold: float = 0.95,
) -> list:
    """Return a list of feature indices to keep."""
    # fit a fast proxy model
    proxy = lgb.LGBMClassifier(n_estimators=100, n_jobs=-1, verbose=-1)
    proxy.fit(X_train, y_train)

    # SHAP-based importance
    explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(proxy)
    shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_train[:500])  # sample for speed
    if isinstance(shap_values, list):
        shap_values = np.abs(shap_values).mean(axis=0)  # multiclass: mean over classes
    importance = np.abs(shap_values).mean(axis=0)

    threshold = np.percentile(importance, importance_threshold_pct)
    keep_mask = importance >= threshold

    # correlation pruning on the surviving features
    X_keep = X_train[:, keep_mask]
    corr_matrix = np.corrcoef(X_keep.T)
    drop_indices = set()
    for i in range(corr_matrix.shape[0]):
        for j in range(i + 1, corr_matrix.shape[1]):
            if abs(corr_matrix[i, j]) > corr_threshold:
                # drop the less important feature of the pair
                if importance[np.where(keep_mask)[0][i]] < importance[np.where(keep_mask)[0][j]]:
                    drop_indices.add(i)
                else:
                    drop_indices.add(j)

    final_indices = [
        idx for i, idx in enumerate(np.where(keep_mask)[0])
        if i not in drop_indices
    ]
    print(f"Feature selection: {X_train.shape[1]} → {len(final_indices)} features kept")
    return final_indices
Real-World Example

A fintech company building a credit-risk model receives daily loan application data. Each batch has slightly different column distributions as the product evolves: new device types, new income verification methods, new geographic markets. A manually maintained preprocessing script required a data scientist to review each new batch and decide whether to update the encoding vocabulary or imputation constants. This took several hours per week and introduced subtle version drift. After replacing the script with a preprocessing agent, each daily retraining job runs the full profiling-planning-fitting cycle automatically. The agent detects when a categorical column's cardinality has grown and switches from OHE to target encoding without human intervention. The serialized Pipeline from each run is registered in MLflow with the dataset fingerprint (SHA-256 of column names and dtypes), allowing any inference request to be replayed against the exact pipeline that was active when the corresponding model was trained.
Common Mistakes
Fitting on the full dataset before splitting.

This is the most common and damaging error. Imputation constants, scaling parameters, and target encoding statistics computed on the full dataset include test-set information, inflating all evaluation metrics.
Target encoding without cross-fold computation.
Encoding each row using the target mean of the same row's fold is a form of label leakage. Always use out-of-fold statistics during training.
Dropping columns with high null rates in all cases.
Sometimes missingness itself is a signal. A null in "income_verified" may predict default risk better than any imputed value. Consider adding a binary indicator column before imputing.
Generating too many engineered features.
Polynomial degree-2 on 100 features produces nearly 5,000 terms. Without a principled selection step downstream, this balloons training time and causes overfitting.
Not saving the pipeline artifact.
Recomputing preprocessing at inference time from code that has since changed produces different transformations, causing silent prediction drift.
Best Practices
Always split before profiling: the agent's profiling loop must read only training rows so test-set statistics cannot influence any transformer
Add a missingness indicator column (col_was_null = 1/0) before imputing numeric columns with more than 5% nulls, then impute so both signals are preserved
Use RobustScaler over StandardScaler when IQR outlier analysis shows heavy-tailed distributions; it scales by median and IQR rather than mean and std
Implement target encoding using sklearn's TargetEncoder (v1.3+), which internally cross-validates on the training set to prevent leakage
Gate feature engineering behind a compute budget: on large datasets, profile a 10% stratified sample and apply the resulting directives to the full set
Register the pipeline artifact in MLflow before model training, not after: if training fails, the pipeline artifact is still available for debugging
Include a schema validation step at inference time that compares incoming feature names and dtypes against the pipeline's expected schema and raises an alert on mismatch
Performance

The profiling step is the most expensive part for wide datasets. Profiling a 1M-row, 200-column DataFrame takes 10–30 seconds depending on dtype mix. For datasets larger than 5M rows, sample 10–20% stratified by target for profiling; apply the resulting transformer decisions to the full set. KNN imputation scales as O(n²) in naive implementations — use sklearn's KNNImputer with n_neighbors=5 and the ball_tree algorithm for datasets above 100k rows, or fall back to median imputation for very large datasets where KNN is prohibitively slow. SHAP importance computation on the feature selection proxy model is the second bottleneck: capping the SHAP background dataset at 500 rows reduces this to a few seconds even for wide feature matrices.
Tooling

The dominant toolkit is sklearn: ColumnTransformer, Pipeline, SimpleImputer, KNNImputer, StandardScaler, RobustScaler, MinMaxScaler, OneHotEncoder, OrdinalEncoder, TargetEncoder (sklearn 1.3+), PolynomialFeatures. For profiling, pandas-profiling (now ydata-profiling) generates comprehensive HTML reports including correlation matrices and distribution plots. Great Expectations provides a contract-based validation layer: you define expectations about column ranges, null rates, and unique counts and the library asserts them at runtime. For heavy feature engineering search, Feature-engine and featuretools offer transformer catalogs and automated deep feature synthesis respectively. Pipeline artifacts are best stored in MLflow Model Registry or DVC-tracked storage. For streaming/online preprocessing, River provides sklearn-compatible incremental transformers that update their statistics with each new observation.
Comparison: Imputation Strategies
Strategy
When to use
Preserves distribution?
Cost
Median imputation
Low null rate (<5%), skewed numeric
No — artificially concentrates at median
O(n log n)
KNN imputation
MAR missingness, 5–30% null rate
Better — uses neighboring rows
O(n²) naive
Iterative imputer (MICE)
MNAR or complex correlations
Best — multivariate conditional
High — multiple passes
Drop column
Null rate >80% and no informational value
N/A
Constant sentinel
MNAR where absence is meaningful (e.g. -1 for "not measured")
No — creates artificial mode
Security and Compliance

Data preprocessing pipelines frequently operate on PII: names, addresses, national identifiers, health records. Before preprocessing, the agent should invoke a PII scanner (e.g., spaCy NER, Presidio) to identify sensitive columns and apply pseudonymization or hashing. Never log raw PII to MLflow or experiment tracking systems. Serialized Pipeline artifacts themselves may embed training-set statistics (e.g., KNN imputation stores training neighbors) — treat them as data assets subject to the same access controls as the underlying dataset. In regulated industries (banking, healthcare) the pipeline version must be auditable: every inference request must be traceable to the exact Pipeline artifact and dataset version that produced the model. DVC + MLflow together provide this chain. Finally, be aware that target encoding leaks the label distribution into feature representations — in adversarial settings this can be exploited to infer approximate label values from feature vectors, so restrict access to encoded feature stores appropriately.
Key Takeaways
Split first, profile second: every statistical decision must be derived from training data only to prevent evaluation metric inflation
A serialized Pipeline artifact is the only reliable way to prevent training-serving skew across the full model lifecycle
Missingness mechanisms (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) determine the right imputation strategy — a blanket median fill is frequently wrong and silently discards signal
Feature engineering must be bounded by a selection step (SHAP, mutual information) or it produces more noise than signal at polynomial scales
Target encoding is powerful for high-cardinality categoricals but requires out-of-fold computation on training data to avoid leakage
Add a schema validation guard at inference time: compare incoming column names and dtypes against the pipeline's expectations and raise an alert before transforming
Registering the pipeline artifact in MLflow before training begins ensures it is preserved even when training fails, enabling complete lineage reconstruction
